item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Source: Wikimedia Commons
Author: Bob Blaylock
Licence: CC BY-SA 3.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a great idea. Someone should do something more with this concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the chemical effects strike me as a bit out of place, this is a very clever concept and such an emotional piece. It has become one of my favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A clever idea paired with very good execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I was editing this myself, I took a look at the history to make sure it wasn't something intentional, and looked at Voct's edits in revision 5, which most were author preference and such, but his spelling of "Millennia" was the correct one, as opposed to "Millenia" with one n. I looked up "Millenia" and only got names of locations, restaurants, music, etc. My guess is that since most of the Voct's edits weren't appropriate, it was reverted despite the Millenia correction, and you just didn't bother re-fixing that. But just in case it is some sort of preference or correct in some dialect, I'll just inform and wait for you, Dr Solo, to fix that yourself, give permission to fix or whatever other alternative.

Edit: Didn't realize the edits were only reverted an hour ago, so apologies if this seemed impatient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except that's not really what's going on. It is not a collection of individual yeasts each contributing to intelligence. It's a singular intelligence that just happens to be living in a bunch of yeasts. It's forgotten things because it is more-or-less on par with a human, so the years have obscured some things. But thanks for your inspiration and help ;P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the line "how old is agriculture" was mine, as is the part about "pieces are missing".

Literally, pieces of this thing's memory are missing, because its memory was held throughout all those cells.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how you credited voct in this, especially since its a LOBOTOMIZED GOD, VOCT YOU'VE STARTED MAKING THEM DO IT TOO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but is otherwise entirely non-anomalous.

oth·er·wise (ˈəT͟Hərˌwīz)
adverb
1. in circumstances different from those present or considered; or else.
2. in other respects; apart from that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm reading an SCP, so yes reading about sentient yeast is perfectly plausible and acceptable. However an SCP that claims that the alcohol produced is non-anomalous and then in the same breath claims that it has anomalous affect which are completely unexplained or even an "huh, that's weird since we just said it was non-anomalous….". It's the continuity break that pulls me out of my suspension of disbelief. So imo its really more trying to fish out the camel so I can drink the gnat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're communicating with a mass of sentient, telepathic yeast, and the unbelievable part is that the alcohol it produces is anomalously intoxicating?

Strain out a gnat, swallow a camel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, I promise that nothing I write is lazy. It's anomalously strong, and that is the anomaly. It doesn't need anything else to be different, because it is anomalous. The whole idea is that it doesn't make sense from an IRL physics perspective, but neither does any other skip on this website. The reason for the anomaly is because it exists, and it is godlike, and it existed to provide for people and maybe garner their worship.

item.title: Marvelous
item.content:encoded: Even if I didn't have a weakness for Greek and Roman myths, this would still get my support. Well-paced and endearing, you gave this being a real emotional voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That just seems kind of lazy considering how much detail and attention went into the rest of the SCP. It says that the alcohol in non anomalous other than being twice as mind affecting. But why? It would have to have some kind of anomalous property in order for it to do that, so it's self contradicting. I'd say take it out or make it have a reason for the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The alcohol is anomalously strong because and anomalous entity has anomalously produced anomalously strong anomalous alcohol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only thing I find a little hard to wrap my head around, is the notion that the alcohol it produces can be more intoxicating. Alcohol is alcohol; other than being more or less concentrated in the liquid as compared to other components(water, sugar, etc). Unless there are other additional excressions that cause side effect intoxications outside of normal alcohol consumption. Normal yeast dies at a concentration maximum of approx 18%. So I would revise to either make the claim that it can survive/tolerate alcohol concentrations up to 36% which would be the equivalent of being twice as strong. The other thing that might be interesting is to have this SCP synthesize a mild form of psilocybin it uses to induce subjects to bring it the desired grains and fruits via subtle telepathic suggestion. This would also explain the additional intoxicating effects of the alcohol produced and spores containing similar compounds could explain the intoxicating effects of the "CO2" excreted as well

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Started reading, got to 'yeast' and went Oh shit, is this Dionysus? :D Glad to finally see it up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thoughtful, and a nice take on the legend without overstating too much. Interviews are believable and add life to the character. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Univine. This was unexpectedly moving. I have been a home brewer (mead, mostly) and baker, so I know a bit more about yeast than I might have otherwise.

It's like the Giving Tree if the rotten little kid never came back at the end. Good for the Foundation for giving it a peaceful place to live out its final years/decades/centuries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is that because the entity is just so god(s)dam old, it was able to watch as humans adapted other gods, real or not. Maybe some were just misunderstood forces of nature like him, but others very well could have been actual for-reals deities

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. I had the same impression from that line. Mother and Father call to mind creator deities and pantheon heads. If you meant that Dio means specifically wine gods, maybe have him rattle off one or two he knows are sourced from him.

Although it didn't bug my headcannon, 'cause it did single handedly (cell-edly?) save its people and assisted in their proliferation, so Dio could reasonably assume it is the source of the very concept of god(s).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There were a couple instances each of "SCP-2263" and "SCP-2563". Since they all referred to this object, I corrected them all to "SCP-2663".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that "I bore witness to the creation of dozens of sky-fathers and divine mothers" could be misinterpreted that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not quite sure where you're getting that implication from. It isn't the source of all myths, just wine-god myths.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to admit that I found the concept of this article a bit far-fetched at first, but I really enjoyed the emotional aspects of this piece, and was really able to sympathize with SCP-2663's loneliness and betrayal by the people it helped. +1 for giving me the feels with a bunch of sentient yeast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only thing I don't like is the implication that this creature is the source of all mythology and gods, as it's pretty clear in-universe that most if not all of the gods worshipped throughout history actually exist(ed) in some form. But I guess the fungus creature could just be unaware of the existence of these other gods and think that the people made them up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You just scippified the invention of beer.
This…this may be brilliance.
+1 for the benevolent beer elemental.

One nitpick:

Carbon dioxide produced by SCP-2663 has several psychological effects when inhaled by humans.

I think you want "exhalations produced by" unless you're meaning it magics up the actual CO2 molecules (Which wouldn't be as good in my book).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hopefully an interesting take on a very old story. I am admittedly not a mycologist or PIE anthropologists, so feel free to correct me on anything.

Special thanks to:
– hide block

MrWrong
Voct
Athenodora
Wogglebug
TL333S
shaggy dreadlocks
greeter
OZ ouroborous
Villager_of_death
Kola

Text taken from the Foundation mission statement in About the scp foundation, written by Aelanna
